title: password_spraying
description: An attacker conducts a password spraying campaign against corporate Office 365 accounts, avoiding lockouts by trying one common password across many users.

timeline.title: SIEM Alert Triggered
timeline.description: SIEM detects over 200 failed authentication attempts spread across 150 accounts from a single external IP within 5 minutes.

timeline.title: Pattern Identified
timeline.description: Security analyst confirms a password spraying pattern — one password being tried against many accounts to evade lockout thresholds.

timeline.title: Accounts Compromised
timeline.description: Four accounts successfully authenticated with the sprayed password. Attacker now has valid credentials.

timeline.title: Lateral Movement Detected
timeline.description: Compromised accounts accessed email and SharePoint. Signs of reconnaissance activity detected in file share audit logs.

timeline.title: Containment Applied
timeline.description: Compromised accounts locked, source IP blocked at the firewall, and MFA enforced for all users.

timeline.title: Post-Incident Review
timeline.description: Forensic review complete. Lessons learned documented and account lockout policies updated.

node-1.title: SIEM Alert: Distributed Login Failures
node-1.situation: The SIEM fires an alert showing over 200 failed login attempts distributed across 150 staff accounts in under 5 minutes, all originating from a single external IP address. No accounts have been locked out yet.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Escalate to the security team, begin investigation of the source IP, and review authentication logs for patterns.

options.label: B
options.text: Dismiss the alert as background noise — failed logins are common.

options.label: C
options.text: Lock only the individual accounts that received failed attempts.

node-2.title: Password Spraying Confirmed
node-2.situation: Analysis confirms this is a password spraying attack. The attacker tried 'Summer2024!' against all accounts. Four accounts successfully authenticated from the attacker's IP and are now showing activity in email and SharePoint.
node-2.question: How do you respond?

options.label: A
options.text: Force an immediate password reset on all four compromised accounts, enable MFA, and block the source IP at the firewall.

options.label: B
options.text: Continue monitoring the attacker's activity before taking action to gather more intelligence.

node-3.title: Data Access Confirmed
node-3.situation: Before being locked out, the attacker accessed email inboxes and browsed a shared HR file store from two of the four compromised accounts. The extent of data access is unknown.
node-3.question: What do you do next?

options.label: A
options.text: Preserve all audit logs, review accessed files for signs of exfiltration, and notify the affected users.

options.label: B
options.text: Close the incident — the attacker has been locked out and is no longer a threat.

node-4.title: Hardening Defenses
node-4.situation: Forensics finds no further lateral movement and no evidence of data being exfiltrated externally. The attack window was limited. Security leadership wants to prevent recurrence.
node-4.question: How do you strengthen defenses?

options.label: A
options.text: Enforce MFA organisation-wide, implement account lockout policies tuned to detect spraying, and deliver targeted staff awareness training on password hygiene.

options.label: B
options.text: Block the attacking IP address and take no further policy action.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: The attack was contained. Compromised accounts were secured, the source IP was blocked, MFA was enforced, and updated lockout policies reduce the risk of future password spraying. A lessons-learned session has been scheduled.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
